dsa_exam_questions.question: Which of the following best describes an array?
options: A. A collection of elements stored at contiguous memory locations
B. A collection of nodes linked by pointers
C. A data structure that follows LIFO
dsa_exam_questions.correct_answer: A. A collection of elements stored at contiguous memory locations
dsa_exam_questions.explanation: An array stores elements of the same type in contiguous memory locations, allowing direct access by index.

dsa_exam_questions.question: What is the index of the first element in a standard C-style array?
dsa_exam_questions.explanation: In C and many programming languages, array indexing starts from 0.

dsa_exam_questions.question: Which operation generally takes O(1) time in an array?
options: C. Accessing an element by index
D. Searching for an unsorted value
dsa_exam_questions.correct_answer: C. Accessing an element by index
dsa_exam_questions.explanation: Arrays support direct random access using the index, which is O(1).

dsa_exam_questions.question: If an array is unsorted, the worst-case time complexity of searching an element is:
dsa_exam_questions.explanation: In an unsorted array, linear search may need to examine all elements.

dsa_exam_questions.question: Which of the following is a major disadvantage of arrays?
options: B. Elements cannot be stored contiguously
C. Fixed size in static implementation
dsa_exam_questions.correct_answer: C. Fixed size in static implementation
dsa_exam_questions.explanation: A static array has a fixed size, which may lead to overflow or memory wastage.

dsa_exam_questions.question: Which traversal visits every element of an array exactly once?
dsa_exam_questions.explanation: Linear traversal iterates through the array from start to end.

dsa_exam_questions.question: Insertion of an element at the end of an array is efficient mainly when:
options: B. There is free space available
D. The first element is NULL
dsa_exam_questions.correct_answer: B. There is free space available
dsa_exam_questions.explanation: Appending is efficient if there is unused capacity and no shifting is required.

dsa_exam_questions.question: Which of the following applications is most suitable for arrays?
dsa_exam_questions.explanation: Arrays are well suited for fixed-size homogeneous collections requiring fast indexed access.

dsa_exam_questions.question: What happens when an insertion is made at the beginning of a full static array?
options: A. It always succeeds in O(1)
B. Existing elements may need shifting and overflow may occur
C. Only the first element is replaced
dsa_exam_questions.correct_answer: B. Existing elements may need shifting and overflow may occur
dsa_exam_questions.explanation: Insertion at the beginning requires shifting; if the array is full, overflow occurs.

dsa_exam_questions.question: Which notation represents the memory address calculation of A[i] in a one-dimensional array?
dsa_exam_questions.explanation: The address of A[i] is calculated from the base address plus offset i times element size.

dsa_exam_questions.question: Which of the following is true about a singly linked list?
options: A. Each node contains data and one link to the next node
B. Each node contains only data
C. Elements are stored in contiguous memory
dsa_exam_questions.correct_answer: A. Each node contains data and one link to the next node
dsa_exam_questions.explanation: A singly linked list node stores data and a pointer to the next node.

dsa_exam_questions.question: Insertion at the beginning of a linked list usually takes:
dsa_exam_questions.explanation: Updating the head pointer is enough, so insertion at the beginning is O(1).

dsa_exam_questions.question: Which linked list allows traversal in both forward and backward directions?
dsa_exam_questions.explanation: A doubly linked list contains both next and previous pointers.

dsa_exam_questions.question: What is the main disadvantage of linked lists compared with arrays?
options: A. More memory is needed for pointers
dsa_exam_questions.correct_answer: A. More memory is needed for pointers
dsa_exam_questions.explanation: Linked lists need extra memory for storing links or pointers.

dsa_exam_questions.question: Which operation is costly in a linked list?
options: C. Random access to the kth node
dsa_exam_questions.correct_answer: C. Random access to the kth node
dsa_exam_questions.explanation: A linked list must be traversed sequentially to reach the kth node.

dsa_exam_questions.question: In a circular linked list, the last node points to:
dsa_exam_questions.explanation: The last node in a circular linked list links back to the first node.

dsa_exam_questions.question: Deleting a node from a singly linked list requires knowledge of:
options: A. Only the deleted node in all cases
B. The previous node in most cases
dsa_exam_questions.correct_answer: B. The previous node in most cases
dsa_exam_questions.explanation: To remove a node, the previous node's next pointer must be updated in a singly linked list.

dsa_exam_questions.question: Which linked list is most suitable for implementing browser back and forward navigation?
dsa_exam_questions.explanation: Backward and forward traversal is efficient in a doubly linked list.

dsa_exam_questions.question: What is the time complexity of searching for an element in an unsorted linked list?
dsa_exam_questions.explanation: The list may need to be traversed node by node until the element is found.

dsa_exam_questions.question: Which statement is correct regarding dynamic memory in linked lists?
options: A. All nodes are allocated at compile time
B. Nodes are typically allocated as needed during runtime
dsa_exam_questions.correct_answer: B. Nodes are typically allocated as needed during runtime
dsa_exam_questions.explanation: Linked lists are dynamic structures and nodes can be created during runtime as needed.

dsa_exam_questions.question: A stack follows which principle?
dsa_exam_questions.explanation: Stack operations follow Last In First Out order.

dsa_exam_questions.question: Which operation inserts an element into a stack?
dsa_exam_questions.explanation: Push inserts an element at the top of the stack.

dsa_exam_questions.question: Which operation removes an element from a stack?
dsa_exam_questions.explanation: Pop removes the top element from the stack.

dsa_exam_questions.question: The overflow condition in an array-based stack occurs when:
dsa_exam_questions.explanation: In a fixed-size stack, overflow occurs when the top index reaches the maximum valid position.

dsa_exam_questions.question: Which application is best suited for a stack?
dsa_exam_questions.explanation: Function calls and recursion are naturally managed using a stack.

dsa_exam_questions.question: A queue follows which principle?
dsa_exam_questions.explanation: Queue operations follow First In First Out order.

dsa_exam_questions.question: Which operation inserts an element into a queue?
dsa_exam_questions.explanation: Enqueue inserts an element at the rear of the queue.

dsa_exam_questions.question: Which operation removes an element from a queue?
dsa_exam_questions.explanation: Dequeue removes an element from the front of the queue.

dsa_exam_questions.question: Which data structure is most suitable for implementing CPU round-robin scheduling?
dsa_exam_questions.explanation: Round-robin scheduling processes jobs in order, making a queue appropriate.

dsa_exam_questions.question: What is the main advantage of a circular queue over a simple linear queue?
options: B. It avoids wastage of unused spaces after deletions
dsa_exam_questions.correct_answer: B. It avoids wastage of unused spaces after deletions
dsa_exam_questions.explanation: A circular queue reuses freed positions, improving memory utilization.

dsa_exam_questions.question: Which data structure is used in breadth-first search?
dsa_exam_questions.explanation: Breadth-first search explores level by level using a queue.

dsa_exam_questions.question: Which operation returns the top element of a stack without deleting it?
dsa_exam_questions.explanation: Peek or top returns the top element without removing it.

dsa_exam_questions.question: A tree is a:
dsa_exam_questions.explanation: A tree represents hierarchical relationships among elements.

dsa_exam_questions.question: The topmost node in a tree is called:
dsa_exam_questions.explanation: The root is the first or topmost node of a tree.

dsa_exam_questions.question: A node with no children is called:
dsa_exam_questions.explanation: A leaf node does not have any children.

dsa_exam_questions.question: What is the maximum number of children a binary tree node can have?
dsa_exam_questions.explanation: Each node in a binary tree can have at most two children.

dsa_exam_questions.question: In a Binary Search Tree, values in the left subtree are:
options: B. Equal to the root only
dsa_exam_questions.explanation: In a BST, left subtree values are smaller and right subtree values are larger than the node.

dsa_exam_questions.question: Searching in a balanced BST has average time complexity:
dsa_exam_questions.explanation: A balanced BST reduces the search space roughly by half at each step.

dsa_exam_questions.question: Which traversal of a BST gives elements in sorted order?
dsa_exam_questions.explanation: Inorder traversal of a BST visits nodes in ascending order.

dsa_exam_questions.question: Preorder traversal visits nodes in which order?
dsa_exam_questions.explanation: Preorder traversal visits root first, then left subtree, then right subtree.

dsa_exam_questions.question: Postorder traversal visits nodes in which order?
dsa_exam_questions.explanation: Postorder traversal visits children before the root.

dsa_exam_questions.question: Which data structure is commonly used to implement recursion internally?
dsa_exam_questions.explanation: Recursive calls are stored in the call stack.

dsa_exam_questions.question: A complete binary tree is one in which:
options: A. Every node has exactly two children
B. All levels are fully filled
C. All levels except possibly the last are full, and last level is filled from left to right
D. It is always a BST
dsa_exam_questions.correct_answer: C. All levels except possibly the last are full, and last level is filled from left to right
dsa_exam_questions.explanation: This is the definition of a complete binary tree.

dsa_exam_questions.question: A max-heap satisfies which property?
options: A. Parent is smaller than both children
B. Parent is greater than or equal to its children
C. Left child is always greater than right child
dsa_exam_questions.correct_answer: B. Parent is greater than or equal to its children
dsa_exam_questions.explanation: In a max-heap, each parent node is greater than or equal to its children.

dsa_exam_questions.question: Which data structure is most suitable for implementing a priority queue?
dsa_exam_questions.explanation: Heaps support efficient insertion and deletion of highest or lowest priority elements.

dsa_exam_questions.question: What is the time complexity of insertion in a binary heap?
dsa_exam_questions.explanation: Insertion may require bubbling up through the heap height, which is logarithmic.

dsa_exam_questions.question: Which traversal uses a queue?
dsa_exam_questions.explanation: Level order traversal processes nodes level by level using a queue.

dsa_exam_questions.question: A skewed BST behaves like:
dsa_exam_questions.explanation: If a BST becomes highly unbalanced, its height approaches n, like a linked list.

dsa_exam_questions.question: The height of a tree with a single node is usually taken as:
dsa_exam_questions.explanation: With height measured in edges, a single-node tree has height 0.

dsa_exam_questions.question: Deleting the root from a max-heap is followed by:
options: B. Replacing it with last element and heapifying
dsa_exam_questions.correct_answer: B. Replacing it with last element and heapifying
dsa_exam_questions.explanation: After deleting the root, the last element is placed at the root and heap property is restored.

dsa_exam_questions.question: A full binary tree is one in which:
options: A. Every level is completely filled
B. Each node has either 0 or 2 children
dsa_exam_questions.correct_answer: B. Each node has either 0 or 2 children
dsa_exam_questions.explanation: A full binary tree has no node with exactly one child.

dsa_exam_questions.question: Which of the following is not necessarily true for a BST?
options: B. Left subtree has smaller keys
D. Search depends on key comparison
dsa_exam_questions.explanation: A BST need not be balanced unless self-balancing rules are used.

dsa_exam_questions.question: Heap sort is based on which data structure?
dsa_exam_questions.explanation: Heap sort builds a heap and repeatedly removes the root element.

dsa_exam_questions.question: A graph consists of:
dsa_exam_questions.explanation: A graph is made up of vertices and the edges connecting them.

dsa_exam_questions.question: In an undirected graph, an edge (u, v) means:
options: A. Travel only from u to v
B. Travel only from v to u
D. No relation between u and v
dsa_exam_questions.explanation: Undirected edges do not have direction.

dsa_exam_questions.question: Which traversal technique generally uses a stack or recursion?
dsa_exam_questions.explanation: Depth-first search uses recursion or an explicit stack.

dsa_exam_questions.question: Which traversal technique explores nearest neighbors first?
dsa_exam_questions.explanation: Breadth-first search explores nodes level by level from the source.

dsa_exam_questions.question: The maximum number of edges in a simple undirected graph with n vertices is:
dsa_exam_questions.explanation: A simple undirected graph can connect each pair of vertices with one edge.

dsa_exam_questions.question: An adjacency matrix for a graph with n vertices requires space:
dsa_exam_questions.explanation: Adjacency matrix stores connection information for all pairs of vertices.

dsa_exam_questions.question: An adjacency list representation is usually more space-efficient for:
dsa_exam_questions.explanation: Adjacency lists are efficient when the number of edges is much less than n^2.

dsa_exam_questions.question: A graph with no cycles is called:
dsa_exam_questions.explanation: Acyclic graphs do not contain any cycle.

dsa_exam_questions.question: Linear search is most suitable when:
options: B. Data size is small or unsorted
D. Data is stored in a BST
dsa_exam_questions.correct_answer: B. Data size is small or unsorted
dsa_exam_questions.explanation: Linear search is simple and useful for small or unsorted collections.

dsa_exam_questions.question: Binary search requires that the array be:
dsa_exam_questions.explanation: Binary search works by dividing an ordered search space.

dsa_exam_questions.question: The worst-case time complexity of binary search is:
dsa_exam_questions.explanation: Binary search halves the search interval on each comparison.

dsa_exam_questions.question: Which sorting algorithm repeatedly compares adjacent elements and swaps them if needed?
dsa_exam_questions.explanation: Bubble sort pushes larger elements toward the end through adjacent swaps.

dsa_exam_questions.question: Which sorting algorithm selects the minimum element and places it at the beginning in each pass?
dsa_exam_questions.explanation: Selection sort repeatedly selects the smallest remaining element.

dsa_exam_questions.question: Insertion sort works efficiently when:
options: B. Data is very large and random
D. Data is stored in a graph
dsa_exam_questions.explanation: Insertion sort performs well on small or nearly sorted datasets.

dsa_exam_questions.question: Which sorting algorithm follows divide-and-conquer and guarantees O(n log n) in all cases?
dsa_exam_questions.explanation: Merge sort divides the array and merges sorted halves with guaranteed O(n log n) time.

dsa_exam_questions.question: Quick sort is generally fast in practice because:
options: C. Its average-case performance is O(n log n)
dsa_exam_questions.correct_answer: C. Its average-case performance is O(n log n)
dsa_exam_questions.explanation: Quick sort has good average-case performance and cache behavior.

dsa_exam_questions.question: Which sort is stable?
dsa_exam_questions.explanation: Merge sort is stable because equal elements preserve relative order during merging.

dsa_exam_questions.question: Heap sort has worst-case time complexity:
dsa_exam_questions.explanation: Heap construction and repeated deletion together yield O(n log n).

dsa_exam_questions.question: The best-case time complexity of linear search is:
dsa_exam_questions.explanation: If the target is at the first position, linear search succeeds immediately.

dsa_exam_questions.question: Which sorting algorithm has worst-case O(n^2) but is simple to implement?
dsa_exam_questions.explanation: Bubble sort is simple but inefficient for large datasets in the worst case.

dsa_exam_questions.question: Binary search compares the target with:
dsa_exam_questions.explanation: Binary search examines the middle element to decide which half to continue searching.

dsa_exam_questions.question: Which sorting algorithm is in-place and usually not stable?
dsa_exam_questions.explanation: Selection sort is in-place but generally not stable.

dsa_exam_questions.question: The average-case time complexity of quick sort is:
dsa_exam_questions.explanation: With reasonably balanced partitions, quick sort runs in O(n log n) on average.

dsa_exam_questions.question: Which search method is best for a sorted linked list?
dsa_exam_questions.explanation: Binary search is inefficient on linked lists because random access is not available.

dsa_exam_questions.question: Which sorting technique inserts each new element into its correct position among the already sorted part?
dsa_exam_questions.explanation: Insertion sort maintains a sorted prefix and inserts the next element appropriately.

dsa_exam_questions.question: Which algorithm is best described as partition-based?
dsa_exam_questions.explanation: Quick sort partitions elements around a pivot.

dsa_exam_questions.question: In selection sort, the number of swaps is generally:
options: A. Very high compared to bubble sort
dsa_exam_questions.explanation: Selection sort usually performs one swap per outer pass after finding the minimum.

dsa_exam_questions.question: Which of these is not a comparison-based sorting algorithm?
dsa_exam_questions.explanation: Counting sort uses key frequencies rather than pairwise comparisons.

dsa_exam_questions.question: Hashing is mainly used for:
options: B. Fast searching based on key
dsa_exam_questions.correct_answer: B. Fast searching based on key
dsa_exam_questions.explanation: Hashing maps keys to table indices for fast lookup.

dsa_exam_questions.question: A collision in hashing occurs when:
options: A. Two keys map to the same index
D. The hash function is recursive
dsa_exam_questions.correct_answer: A. Two keys map to the same index
dsa_exam_questions.explanation: A collision happens when different keys produce the same hash value or slot.

dsa_exam_questions.question: Which of the following is a collision resolution technique?
dsa_exam_questions.explanation: Chaining stores multiple elements in the same bucket using a linked structure.

dsa_exam_questions.question: What is load factor in hashing?
options: B. Ratio of filled slots to total slots
D. Size of linked list only
dsa_exam_questions.correct_answer: B. Ratio of filled slots to total slots
dsa_exam_questions.explanation: Load factor indicates how full a hash table is.

dsa_exam_questions.question: Recursion is a technique in which:
options: A. A function calls another unrelated function only
B. A function calls itself directly or indirectly
dsa_exam_questions.correct_answer: B. A function calls itself directly or indirectly
dsa_exam_questions.explanation: Recursion solves a problem by reducing it to smaller instances of itself.

dsa_exam_questions.question: Every recursive solution should have:
dsa_exam_questions.explanation: Without a base case, recursive calls may continue indefinitely.

dsa_exam_questions.question: Which data structure supports recursion during execution?
dsa_exam_questions.explanation: Each recursive call is pushed onto the call stack.

dsa_exam_questions.question: The time complexity of accessing an element in a hash table is O(1) on average when:
options: B. A good hash function is used and load factor is managed
dsa_exam_questions.correct_answer: B. A good hash function is used and load factor is managed
dsa_exam_questions.explanation: Average constant-time performance depends on uniform distribution and controlled load factor.

dsa_exam_questions.question: Big-O notation describes:
options: C. Upper bound growth rate of an algorithm
dsa_exam_questions.correct_answer: C. Upper bound growth rate of an algorithm
dsa_exam_questions.explanation: Big-O expresses asymptotic upper bound on time or space growth.

dsa_exam_questions.question: Which notation gives a tight asymptotic bound?
dsa_exam_questions.explanation: Theta represents both upper and lower asymptotic bound together.

dsa_exam_questions.question: Which of the following grows fastest for large n?
dsa_exam_questions.explanation: Quadratic growth dominates linearithmic, linear, and logarithmic growth for large n.

dsa_exam_questions.question: The complexity of traversing all elements of an array of size n is:
dsa_exam_questions.explanation: Each element is visited once, so time grows linearly.

dsa_exam_questions.question: Which complexity is typical for nested loops each running n times?
dsa_exam_questions.explanation: A loop inside another loop with n iterations each performs roughly n*n operations.

dsa_exam_questions.question: Which statement about O(1) is correct?
options: B. Runtime does not depend on input size asymptotically
C. It is always faster than O(log n) in real life
D. It means one CPU cycle
dsa_exam_questions.correct_answer: B. Runtime does not depend on input size asymptotically
dsa_exam_questions.explanation: O(1) means constant-time growth with respect to input size, not literally one step.

dsa_exam_questions.question: Which of the following algorithms typically uses recursion?
dsa_exam_questions.explanation: Binary search, merge sort, and quick sort are commonly expressed recursively.

dsa_exam_questions.question: What is the worst-case time complexity of quick sort?
dsa_exam_questions.explanation: Quick sort degrades to quadratic time with highly unbalanced partitions.

dsa_exam_questions.question: Open addressing in hashing stores collided items:
options: B. In another open slot within the table
dsa_exam_questions.correct_answer: B. In another open slot within the table
dsa_exam_questions.explanation: Open addressing resolves collisions by probing for another empty slot.

dsa_exam_questions.question: Which probing technique checks the next slot sequentially?
dsa_exam_questions.explanation: Linear probing checks subsequent positions one by one until an empty slot is found.

dsa_exam_questions.question: The recursion depth for binary search on n elements is about:
dsa_exam_questions.explanation: Each recursive call halves the input size, leading to logarithmic depth.
